curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Measure and use bearings as three-figure angles clockwise from North, applying them accurately in navigation-style problems and reflecting on real-world tolerances.

verse_ality.driving_question: How can we use bearings (measured clockwise from North) to navigate accurately in the real world?

knowing_world.focus: Learn how bearings are measured clockwise from North and written as three digits, then practise reading and setting bearings precisely.

human_story.focus: Explore how bearings supported exploration and trade as people navigated oceans and deserts using stars and simple tools to measure direction.

expressive_self.focus: Reflect on how reading a compass can build independence and confidence in outdoor settings such as hiking and orienteering.
activities: Write a short reflection: How could compass skills support your independence?
Describe a scenario (real or imagined) where correct bearings would matter.

designed_world.focus: Connect bearings to modern systems including architecture, engineering, aviation, and navigation apps, where direction and angles remain foundational.
design_challenges: Find one modern technology that uses direction/angles (e.g., GPS navigation) and explain how bearings relate.
Design a simple navigation challenge (start point + target bearing) for someone else to try.

living_body.focus: Use mental rotation and spatial reasoning to strengthen cognitive mapping skills by imagining the compass in different orientations and scenarios.
regulation_supports: Pause and visualise North before setting each bearing.
Mentally rotate the compass and predict where the arrow should point before checking.

pathways.name: Final Task: Set and Match Bearings
pathways.focus: Use the interactive compass to match target bearings accurately, then reflect on strategies and real-world tolerance.
products: Evidence of at least 3 successful bearing matches (e.g., screenshots or a written record).
A short description of strategies you used to match bearings reliably.
A response explaining why a small margin of error (e.g., ±5°) can be acceptable in some real-world applications.

required: Basic compass directions (N, E, S, W)
